en: Which has been the most powerful intelligence agency in history?
ko: 역사상 가장 강력했던 정보기관은 어디인가요

en: The most powerful intelligence agency in history.
ko: 역사상 가장 강력했던 정보기관은 어디인가요

en: I mean, it's an interesting question.
ko: 역사상 가장 강력한 정보기관이라, 흥미로운 질문이네요

en: I'd say probably in terms of historical longevity and consistency of performance, that the Russian
ko: 꾸준한 성과 면에서 보면

en: intelligence services. Notice I didn't say the KGB specifically, but the Russian intelligence services, going
ko: 러시아 정보기관이라고 하겠습니다 KGB가 아니라 러시아

en: back to the czarist period, are consistently pretty good.
ko: 정보기관 전체를 말하는 건데, 차르 시대까지 거슬러 올라가면 꾸준히 뛰어났습니다

en: Not infallible. None of them are.
ko: 완벽하진 않죠 어떤 기관도 완벽하진 않습니다

en: Of course, there's a common Western way of looking at anything Russian.
ko: 물론 서방에서 러시아를 바라보는 전형적인 시각이 있죠

en: Very often, I think it's still the case, Russians are viewed in one of two ways.
ko: 아직도 그렇다고 봅니다 러시아인은 두 가지로 인식돼요 멍청한

en: they are bumbling idiots, or they are diabolically clever.
ko: 바보이거나 악마적으로 영리하거나

en: No sort of middle ground. You can find both of those examples in this.
ko: 중간이 없어요 둘 다 사례를 찾을 수 있습니다

en: So what I mean by that is that if you're looking at the modern SVR or FSB, which are just two
ko: 제 말은, 현대 SVR이나 FSB를 보면

en: different organizations that used to be part of the one big KGB, or the KGB or its predecessors, there's the Cheka.
ko: 원래 하나였던 KGB에서 나뉜 두 조직이죠, KGB나 그 전신인

en: You're really going back to the late 19th century and the Imperial Russian Intelligence
ko: 사실 19세기 후반 제정 러시아

en: Security Service, generally known as the Okrana or Okranka.
ko: 보통 오흐라나라고 불리는

en: It's really the Department of Police, the special corps of gendarmes.
ko: 실제로는 경찰국 특별헌병대입니다 주요 임무는

en: Their primary job was protecting the imperial regime and protecting it against imperial
ko: 실제로는 경찰국 특별헌병대입니다 주요 임무는

en: or other interior enemies, revolutionaries for the most part.
ko: 제정 체제를 보호하고 내부 적들로부터 방어하는 것이었습니다

en: And they got very, very good at that by co-opting people within those movements, infiltrating
ko: 운동 조직 안에요

en: and recruiting informers, agent provocateurs.
ko: 침투하고 밀고자를 모집하고, 도발 공작원을 심었어요 특히 뛰어났던 건

en: In fact, they excelled at the agent provocateur.
ko: 침투하고 밀고자를 모집하고, 도발 공작원을 심었어요 특히 뛰어났던 건

en: Person you place inside an organization to cause trouble, usually maneuver them into
ko: 도발 공작원이었습니다 조직 안에 심어서 문제를 일으키는 사람이죠

en: a position of leadership, and they provoke actions that can then allow you to crack down
ko: 보통 지도부까지 올려놓고 행동을 유발합니다

en: on them.
That is, many sort of lure or bring the target organization into any legal or open status
ko: 그러면 단속할 수 있게 되죠 목표 조직을 끌어내서

en: that it can be more effectively suppressed. They were very good at that.
So good that by the early 20th century, in the years preceding the Russian Revolution
ko: 정말 잘했어요 20세기 초

en: in 1917, they had effectively infiltrated every radical party, Bolsheviks, Mensheviks,
ko: 1917년 러시아 혁명 전까지 모든 급진 정당에 침투했습니다

en: SRs, great and small, and placed people in positions of influence and leadership.
ko: 크고 작은 조직에 영향력 있는 자리에 사람들을 배치했죠

en: To the point that arguably, that is, you can debate this, but I think in the whole, they
ko: 논쟁의 여지는 있지만 전체적으로 봤을 때

en: could largely dictate what those parties did.
ko: 그 정당들의 행동을 거의 좌지우지할 수 있었습니다

en: Nothing was discussed at any central committee meeting of any revolutionary group that the
ko: 그 정당들의 행동을 거의 좌지우지할 수 있었습니다

en: Okrana wasn't immediately aware of.
ko: 어떤 혁명 조직의 중앙위원회 회의도 오흐라나가 모르는 게 없었어요

en: And they often had people in positions to influence what those decisions were.
ko: 게다가 결정에 영향을 줄 수 있는 위치에 사람을 심어뒀죠

en: Of course, that raises an interesting question, is that if they were that good and they had
ko: 물론 흥미로운 질문이 생기죠 그렇게 뛰어나고 대부분의

en: infiltrated and effectively controlled most of the opposition, then how did the regime
ko: 물론 흥미로운 질문이 생기죠 그렇게 뛰어나고 대부분의

en: get overthrown by revolutionaries?
ko: 반대 세력을 장악했다면 어떻게 혁명가들한테 무너졌느냐

en: The answer to that is that it wasn't overthrown by revolutionaries.
ko: 반대 세력을 장악했다면 어떻게 혁명가들한테 무너졌느냐

en: It was overthrown by politicians.
ko: 답은 혁명가들이 아니라 정치인들이 무너뜨렸다는 겁니다

en: That would then take us into a detour into Russian history.
ko: 답은 혁명가들이 아니라 정치인들이 무너뜨렸다는 겁니다

en: But I'll just leave it with this.
ko: 러시아 역사 이야기가 되겠지만 이것만 말씀드리죠

en: If you look at 1917 and you look closely, this is one of the things I would always tell
ko: 러시아 역사 이야기가 되겠지만 이것만 말씀드리죠

en: my students, is that there are two Russian revolutions in 1917.
ko: 1917년을 자세히 보면, 항상 학생들에게 말하는 건데

en: There's the first one in March or February, depending on your calendar, that overthrows Nicholas II.
ko: 니콜라이 2세를 무너뜨린 거죠

en: Revolutionaries are really not involved with that.
ko: 혁명가들은 거기 관여하지 않았어요 볼셰비키는 보이지도 않았고 트로츠키와 레닌도

en: Bolsheviks are nowhere to be seen. Trotsky and Lenin are nowhere to be seen.
ko: 혁명가들은 거기 관여하지 않았어요 볼셰비키는 보이지도 않았고 트로츠키와 레닌도

en: They have nothing to do with that. That has to do effectively with a political conspiracy within the Russian parliament,
ko: 그건 러시아 의회 두마 내의 정치적 음모였어요

en: the Duma, to unseat an emperor they thought was bungling the war and was essentially a
ko: 그건 러시아 의회 두마 내의 정치적 음모였어요

en: loser to begin with. And it was a coup d'etat, a parliamentary coup d'etat.
ko: 쿠데타, 의회 쿠데타였습니다

en: The temporary or provisional government that that revolution put in power was the one overthrown
ko: 그 혁명으로 들어선 임시정부가

en: by Lenin eight months later.
ko: 8개월 후 레닌에게 무너진 겁니다

en: And that government was essentially one dominated by moderate socialists.
ko: 그 정부는 온건 사회주의자들이 지배했고

en: It was a government that very quickly sort of turned to the left.
ko: 빠르게 좌경화됐습니다

en: The guy we associate with that is Alexander Kerensky.
ko: 우리가 떠올리는 인물은 케렌스키입니다 러시아

en: Alexander Kerensky was a Russian socialist, a politician.
ko: 우리가 떠올리는 인물은 케렌스키입니다 러시아

en: He was the quasi-dictator of that regime.
ko: 사회주의자이자 정치인으로 그 정권의 준독재자였죠

en: He's the person, not the czar, who's overthrown by Lenin.
ko: 레닌에게 무너진 건 차르가 아니라 그였습니다

en: So the revolutionaries, they did not prove to be the fatal threat to the czarist regime.
ko: 혁명가들은 차르 체제의 치명적 위협이 아니었어요

en: It was the czarist political system itself that did that.
ko: 차르 체제 자체가 스스로를 무너뜨린 겁니다

en: What then transpired was that the Okhrana and its method and many of its agents then
ko: 이후 오흐라나와 그 방법론, 많은 요원들이 곧바로

en: immediately segued over into the new Soviet security service.
ko: 이후 오흐라나와 그 방법론, 많은 요원들이 곧바로

en: So one of the first things that Lenin did in December of 1917, within a month of seizing
ko: 새 소비에트 보안기관으로 넘어갔습니다 레닌이 1917년 12월

en: power, since the hold on power was tenuous at best, was that, well, you're going to need
ko: 권력 장악이 불안정했으니까

en: some kind of organization to infiltrate and suppress those pesky counter-revolutionaries
ko: 성가신 반혁명분자들과 외국 제국주의자들을 침투하고 진압할 조직이 필요하다는 거였죠

en: and foreign imperialists and all of the other enemies that we have.
ko: 성가신 반혁명분자들과 외국 제국주의자들을 침투하고 진압할 조직이 필요하다는 거였죠

en: So the extraordinary commission to combat counter-revolution and sabotage, the Cheka,
ko: 그래서 반혁명 및 사보타주 방지 비상위원회

en: was formed.
You put a veteran Bolshevik, Felix Dzerzhinsky, at the head of that, someone you could politically
ko: 노련한 볼셰비키 펠릭스 제르진스키를

en: rely upon, but Dzerzhinsky built his organization essentially out of the Okhrana.
ko: 수장으로 앉혔죠 정치적으로 믿을 수 있는 사람이었지만 제르진스키는 조직을

en: I mean, there were all of these informers sitting around with nothing to do, and they
ko: 오흐라나 출신으로 구성했어요 할 일 없이 놀던 밀고자들이 잔뜩 있었고

en: were employed.
ko: 그들을 고용한 거죠

en: In the early 20s, the kind of rank and file of the Cheka might have been 80% to 90% former
ko: 1920년대 초 체카의 일반 요원 중 80~90%가

en: imperial officials. Those were gradually decreased over time.
ko: 전 제국 관리였습니다 시간이 지나면서 줄어들었지만

en: So why would they do that? Well, they were professionals. They also needed to eat, and things were somewhat precarious.
ko: 왜 그랬냐면 프로였으니까요 밥도 먹어야 하고 상황이 불안정했으니

en: So if your job is to be an agent provocateur, if your job is to infiltrate targeted organizations
ko: 도발 공작원이 직업이고 목표 조직에 침투하는 게 일이면

en: and lead them astray, you do that for whoever pays you.
ko: 돈 주는 사람을 위해 하는 거죠

en: That's part of the professionalism which goes in.
ko: 그게 전문성의 일부입니다

en: And under the Soviets, the Soviet intelligence services are also very good at that.
ko: 소비에트 하에서도 정보기관은 이런 데 뛰어났어요

en: They're very good at infiltrating people into opposing organizations.
ko: 소비에트 하에서도 정보기관은 이런 데 뛰어났어요

en: And I guess the one example I would give to demonstrate that are the Cambridge Five, the
ko: 케임브리지 5인조입니다

en: British traitors from a Soviet standpoint, heroes who were recruited, most notably Kim
ko: 영국인 배신자들, 소련 관점에서는 영웅들이죠

en: Philby, Guy Burgess, Donald MacLean, Anthony Blunt.
ko: 가장 유명한 건 킴 필비, 가이 버제스, 도널드 맥클린

en: And there may have been well more than five, but that wasn't bad out of just Cambridge.
ko: 케임브리지 출신치고는 대단했죠

en: And then placing those people in high positions, the ultimate goal, of course, is to get your
ko: 그리고 고위직에 배치하는 거죠 궁극적 목표는

en: people into positions of leadership and influence in the opposing intelligence service.
ko: 적 정보기관의 지도부에 사람을 심는 겁니다

en: And so they did.
ko: 실제로 그랬죠 물론 결국 무너졌고

en: Of course, it all fell apart, and they ended up in, you know, Philby ended up living the
ko: 실제로 그랬죠 물론 결국 무너졌고

en: last part of his life in exile in Moscow, but they got their money's worth out of him.
ko: 충분히요

en: And you can also find this in KGB infiltration of the CIA, the FBI, the Aldrich Ames, the
ko: CIA나 FBI에 대한 KGB 침투에서도 볼 수 있어요 올드리치 에임스 사건이죠

en: we were infiltrating by we, I mean, the Americans in the West managed to infiltrate our moles
ko: 우리도, 미국과 서방도 우리 쪽에

en: as well. But if it came down, you know, someone could dispute this, but I would think if you were
going to come down to a kind of like a who had the most moles Super Bowl, probably the
ko: 슈퍼볼 같은 걸 하면

en: Soviets would come somewhat ahead of that.
ko: 아마 소련이 약간

en: So the scale of the infiltration, the number of people and the skill of it.
ko: 앞설 겁니다 침투의 규모, 인원, 기술 면에서

en: Is there a case to be made that the Okhrana and the Chaka orchestrated both the components
ko: 오흐라나와

en: of the Russian Revolution as you described them? Well, there's an interesting question for me.
I mean, there are all kinds of questions about this.
ko: 흥미로운 질문이죠 여러 의문이 있는데 하나는

en: I mean, one of the questions is whether or not Lenin was an Okhrana agent.
ko: 흥미로운 질문이죠 여러 의문이 있는데 하나는

en: Okay, I've just said heresy.
ko: 레닌이 오흐라나 요원이었느냐입니다 이단적인 말이죠

en: Some people will, I'll do that quite often because I am a heretic and proud of it.
ko: 저는 자주 그러는데 이단아이고 자랑스러워합니다

en: Great. Um, why, why would you possibly say that Lenin could have been an Okhrana agent?
ko: 왜 레닌이 오흐라나 요원이었을 수 있다고 하시는 건가요

en: Well, let's look what he managed to do.
ko: 그가 무엇을 해냈는지 봅시다

en: So you had coming into the 20th century, a, a single L nominally, a single Marxist movement,
ko: 20세기에 들어서면서

en: the Russian social democratic labor party.
ko: 명목상 단일 마르크스주의 운동이 있었어요 러시아 사회민주

en: And Bolsheviks and Mensheviks, majorityites and minorityites are merely factions of that
ko: 노동당이었고 볼셰비키와 멘셰비키는

en: party.
And they always agreed that they were all Marxists and we all believe in dialectical
ko: 그저 당내 파벌이었습니다 항상 동의했죠

en: materialism and the rise of, so we're all, we're all socialist comrade.
ko: 우리 모두 사회주의자야, 동지

en: The difference was the, the tactical means by which one would attain this.
ko: 차이는 목표를 달성하는 전술적 방법이었습니다

en: And, uh, what Lenin wanted was a, a militant, small-scale vanguard party, wanted a revolution,
ko: 레닌이 원한 건 전투적인 소규모 전위당이었고

en: wanted to seize power, seize control of the state.
ko: 혁명을 일으켜 권력을 잡으려 했죠

en: And once you have the state, then you induce socialism from above.
ko: 사회주의를 도입하는 겁니다

en: Whereas the majority of the people, the so-called Mensheviks, the minorityites, who are oddly
ko: 반면 다수파, 소수파라 불리는 멘셰비키는

en: enough, the vast majority of the party, I, that's one of the first things, how do you lose that argument?
ko: 이상하게도 당의 압도적 다수였죠 어떻게 그 논쟁에서 지는 거냐면

en: Okay. How did, how did the, how does the minority get to grab the name majorityites, but Lenin did that.
ko: 소수가 어떻게 다수파란 이름을 뺏었냐, 레닌이 그걸 해냈죠

en: So what Lenin wanted was a, a conspiratorial party of committed revolutionaries that would
ko: 레닌이 원한 건 헌신적인 혁명가들의 비밀 정당이었습니다

en: plot and scheme and undermine and eventually seize control of the state and induce socialism from above.
ko: 음모를 꾸미고 약화시키고 결국 국가를 장악해서 위로부터 사회주의를 도입하는

en: There were other Russian Marxists who thought that that sounded vaguely totalitarian and
ko: 다른 러시아 마르크스주의자들은 그게 전체주의적이고 민주적이지 않다고 봤죠

en: not really democratic and not even terribly socialist.
ko: 사회주의적이지도 않다고요

en: And they opposed that ineffectively from the beginning, outmaneuvered every step of the
ko: 처음부터 비효과적으로 반대했습니다

en: way. The Mensheviks are a case study in failure of a political organization.
ko: 멘셰비키는 정치 조직 실패의 교과서적 사례입니다

en: That too will be heresy to some people, but look, they lost.
ko: 이단이겠지만 그들은 졌어요

en: Now, so what Lenin managed to do starting around 1903, continuing onto this, is he managed
ko: 1903년경부터 레닌이 해낸 건

en: to divide, to take what had been a single Marxist party and split it into angry contending
ko: 하나였던 마르크스주의 정당을 분노한 대립하는

en: factions, because he and his Bolsheviks were on one side advocating a much more militant
ko: 파벌들로 쪼갰어요 그와 볼셰비키는 훨씬 전투적이고 비밀스러운

en: conspiratorial policy.
ko: 노선을 주장했고

en: The discombobulated Mensheviks were over on the other, and in between were a lot of people
ko: 혼란에 빠진 멘셰비키는 반대편에, 중간에는 어디에 서야 할지

en: who really didn't know where they stood on this.
ko: 혼란에 빠진 멘셰비키는 반대편에, 중간에는 어디에 서야 할지

en: I mean, sometimes they kind of agreed, and he seems to be making sense today.
ko: 모르는 사람들이 많았죠 오늘은 맞는 것 같다가도 아닌 것 같고

en: No, no, I don't think he's making sense in that day, but he managed to completely disunify
ko: 모르는 사람들이 많았죠 오늘은 맞는 것 같다가도 아닌 것 같고

en: this organization. Now, who could possibly have seen benefit in that?
ko: 결국 조직을 완전히 분열시켰어요 누가

en: The ograna.
Now, whether or not they put him up to it, whether or not in some way they helped move
ko: 그걸로 이득을 봤겠냐 오흐라나죠

en: him into a position of leadership, or encouraged it, or encouraged it through people around
ko: 그들이 시켰는지, 지도자 위치로 밀어줬는지

en: him, whether he was a witting or unwitting agent of the Tsarist secret police, he certainly
ko: 차르 비밀경찰의 요원이었는지

en: accomplished exactly what it was that they had wanted.
ko: 분명 그들이 원하던 걸 정확히 이뤄냈어요

en: And I find that suspicious.
ko: 의심스럽습니다

en: It's one of those things that it's so convenient in a way that I'm not necessarily sure that
ko: 너무 편리해서

en: was an accident.
ko: 우연이 아닐 수도 있다는 거죠

en: There's also this whole question to me as to what was going on within the ograna itself.
ko: 오흐라나 내부에서 무슨 일이 있었느냐는 질문도 있습니다

en: Now, this is one of these questions we may come to later about how intelligence agencies
ko: 나중에 다룰 수 있는 질문인데 정보기관이

en: interact or serve the governments to which they are theoretically subordinate.
ko: 이론적으로 종속된 정부를 어떻게 섬기느냐는 것이죠

en: They do tend to acquire a great deal of influence and power.
ko: 상당한 영향력과 권력을 축적하는 경향이 있어요

en: After all, their main job is to collect information, and that information could be about all kinds
ko: 주요 임무가 정보 수집이고 그 정보는 온갖 것에 대한 것일 수 있죠

en: of things, including people within the government structure itself.
ko: 정부 내부 인사에 대한 정보도 포함해서요

en: And they also know how to leverage that information in a way to get people to do what you want
ko: 그 정보를 활용해서 원하는 대로 움직이게 하는 법도 알고 있습니다

en: them to do.
ko: 해

en: So an argument can be made, again, an argument, not a fact, merely an opinion, which is mostly
ko: 논거를 제시할 수 있어요 사실이 아니라 의견입니다

en: what history is made out of, opinions, is that at some point between about 1900 and
ko: 역사는 대부분 의견으로 이뤄져 있죠 어느 시점에

en: 1917, people within the ograna were playing their own game.
ko: 1900년에서 1917년 사이에 오흐라나 내부 사람들이 자기들만의 게임을 했다는 겁니다

en: And that game took them in a direction which meant that continued loyalty to the emperor,
ko: 그 게임은 황제에 대한 충성이

en: specifically to Nicholas II, was no longer part of that.
ko: 더 이상 포함되지 않는 방향이었죠

en: To me, in a way, it seems almost during the events of 1917 that, one, you had an organization
ko: 1917년 사건들을 보면 매우 효과적이던 조직이

en: that was very effective when it did, that suddenly just becomes ineffective, doesn't
ko: 갑자기 무력해지는 게 이상해요

en: really disappear, these things don't go away, because it will reappear as the Ocheka, basically,
ko: 사라지지 않죠 이런 건 없어지지 않아요 체카로 다시 나타나니까

en: fairly quickly.
ko: 상당히 빨리요

en: But it raises the question to me as to what degree there were people within the organization
ko: 조직 내부에 어느 정도까지

en: who allowed events to take the course they wished.
ko: 원하는 방향으로 사건이 흘러가도록 내버려 둔 사람들이 있었느냐는 질문이죠

en: I always wonder how much deliberate planning there is within an organization like ograna,
ko: 항상 궁금한 게 조직 내에 얼마나 의도적인 계획이 있었느냐

en: or if there's kind of a distributed intelligence that happens.
ko: 아니면 분산된 형태로 일어나는 건지

en: Well, one of the key elements in any kind of intelligence organization or operation
ko: 정보 조직이나 작전의 핵심 요소 중 하나는

en: is compartmentalization, need to know.
ko: 구획화입니다 알 필요가 있는 사람만 아는 거죠

en: So rarely do you have an occasion where everybody, everybody in an executive position are all
ko: 임원급 전원이 모여서 진행 중인 비밀 작전을 논의하는 일은 거의 없어요

en: brought into a big corporate meeting and we discuss all of the secret operations that are going on.
ko: 절대 그러지 않습니다

en: No, no, you never do that.
ko: 절대 그러지 않습니다

en: Only a very limited number of people should know about that.
ko: 극소수만 알아야 해요

en: If you have a person who is a case officer who's controlling agents, he's the only one
ko: 극소수만 알아야 해요

en: who should know who those people are, possibly his immediate superiors, but in no way do
ko: 담당관만 그 요원들이 누군지 알아야 하고 기껏해야

en: you want that to be common knowledge.
ko: 직속 상관 정도지 절대 공개되면 안 됩니다

en: So information within the organization itself is compartmentalized.
ko: 조직 내부의 정보도 구획화되어 있습니다

en: So you don't need everybody to be in on it.
ko: 전원이 알 필요 없고 명목상 최고 책임자도

en: You don't even need necessarily the people who are nominally at the top.
ko: 전원이 알 필요 없고 명목상 최고 책임자도

en: For instance, the ograna, the real boss of the ograna was the imperial ministry of the
ko: 알 필요 없어요 오흐라나의 실질적 상관은 제국 내무부였는데

en: interior, the minister of the interior, in fact, but minister of the interior had no
ko: 인테리어

en: real effective control over this at all.
ko: 내무장관이었지만 실질적 통제력은 전혀 없었어요

en: I mean, to the point was that at one point early on, they actually organized the assassination
ko: 내무장관이었지만 실질적 통제력은 전혀 없었어요

en: of their own boss.
ko: 초기에 자기 상관의 암살을 조직하기까지 했으니까요

en: They have their agents among the revolutionaries kill the minister of the interior because
ko: 혁명가들 속 요원을 시켜 내무장관을 죽였어요

en: he'll just be replaced by another one. He is an imperial bureaucrat.
ko: 어차피 다른 사람으로 교체되니까 제국 관료일 뿐 자기 조직원이 아닌 거죠

en: He's not really part of their organization.
ko: 어차피 다른 사람으로 교체되니까 제국 관료일 뿐 자기 조직원이 아닌 거죠

en: It's like a director of an intelligence agency appointed by the president.
ko: 대통령이 임명한 정보기관장 같은 거예요

en: Maybe he's part of the organization. Maybe he isn't.
ko: 조직의 일원일 수도 있고 아닐 수도 있고 우리 편이 아닐 수도 있죠

en: Maybe he is not one of us.
ko: 조직의 일원일 수도 있고 아닐 수도 있고 우리 편이 아닐 수도 있죠

en: So you've got different levels, different compartments within it, and who's actually
ko: 여러 단계가 있는 겁니다

en: running the show, if anyone is, I don't know.
ko: 서로 다른 구획이 있고 누가 실제로 운영하는지 모릅니다

en: That's never supposed to be apparent. Well, that's a fascinating question, and you can see this with NKVD.
ko: 드러나면 안 되는 거니까요 NKVD에서도 볼 수 있죠

en: It's obviously an extremely powerful organization that starts to eat itself, where everybody's
ko: 엄청나게 강력한 조직이

en: pointing fingers internally also as a way to gain more power.
ko: 권력을 얻으려고

en: So the question is, in organizations like that that are so compartmentalized, where's the power?
ko: 그런 구획화된 조직에서 권력이 어디 있느냐

en: Where's the center of power?
ko: 그런 구획화된 조직에서 권력이 어디 있느냐

en: Because you would think given that much power, some individual or a group of individuals
ko: 권력의 중심이 어디냐 그만큼의 권력이면

en: will start accumulating that power, but it seems like that's not always a trivial thing
ko: 누군가 권력을 축적하기 시작할 텐데 항상 그런 건 아닌 것 같아요

en: because if you get too powerful, the snake eats that person.
ko: 너무 강해지면 뱀이 그 사람을 잡아먹으니까

en: Well, if we go back into the founder of Soviet secret police, Felix Dzerzhinsky, Dzerzhinsky
ko: 소비에트 비밀경찰 창설자 제르진스키로 돌아가면

en: dies in 1926, keels over after giving a heated speech to a party meeting.
ko: 당 회의에서 격앙된 연설을 한 뒤 쓰러졌죠

en: Now the common view, what you usually read, which was clear for the time, is that clearly
ko: 당시 통념은 스탈린이 죽였다는 거예요

en: Stalin had him whacked because anytime someone died, it was almost always that, and I think
ko: 당시 통념은 스탈린이 죽였다는 거예요

en: a lot of times he did.
ko: 누가 죽으면 거의 항상 스탈린 탓이었고 실제로 많은 경우 그랬죠

en: But in some cases, Stalin's probably getting blamed for things that he didn't actually do.
ko: 하지만 때로는 안 한 일까지 뒤집어 쓰기도 했을 겁니다

en: Dzerzhinsky wasn't even opposed to Stalin, so it's not clear why he would, but this was
ko: 제르진스키는 스탈린 반대파도 아니었는데

en: the, you know, Stalin died, you know, obviously he was poisoned, something happened, it was an unnatural death.
ko: 누가 죽으면 당연히 독살이다, 뭔가 일어났다, 비자연사라는 식이었죠

en: Somebody goes in for an operation, you know, gets a little too much anesthesia, Stalin killed them.
ko: 수술 가서 마취가 좀 과하면 스탈린이 죽인 거다

en: Somebody tips over in a canoe in upstate New York, Stalin killed them, there's actually
ko: 뉴욕 주 북부에서 카누가 뒤집혀도

en: a case about that.
ko: 스탈린이 죽인 거다 실제로 그런 사건이 있어요

en: So that itself can be kind of useful where every time someone dies, they think you killed
ko: 그것 자체가 유용할 수 있어요 누가 죽을 때마다 당신이 죽였다고 생각하면

en: them. That's kind of an interesting method of intimidation in that regard.
ko: 일종의 위협 수단이 되는 거죠

en: But the suspicion is nonetheless there, Dzerzhinsky had been, he was the grand inquisitor.
ko: 하지만 의심은 남아 있죠 제르진스키는

en: He was seemingly firmly in control of the organization, of course, maybe he wasn't,
ko: 대심문관이었고 조직을 확실히 장악한 것 같았지만

en: maybe he was, my guess would be is that if Dzerzhinsky's death was not natural causes,
ko: 아닐 수도 있죠 제 추측으로는 자연사가 아니었다면

en: that he was probably eliminated by someone within his own organization.
ko: 자기 조직 내부 누군가가 제거했을 겁니다

en: And then you look at the people who take over, his immediate successor is Vyacheslav Menzhinsky,
ko: 이후 후임자들을 보면

en: who's really not really a secret policeman, more a kind of intellectual dilettante.
ko: 비밀경찰이라기보다 지적 호사가였죠

en: But if you look behind him, you'll notice the fellow is Henrikh Yagoda.
ko: 하지만 뒤를 보면 야고다가 있었습니다

en: And Yagoda will really sort of manage things from behind the scenes until Menzhinsky dies
ko: 야고다가 멘진스키가 1934년 죽을 때까지 뒤에서 관리했고

en: And then Yagoda will hold on until he's a victim of the purges, I think in 37 or 38.
ko: 야고다는 37년이나 38년쯤 숙청으로 제거됐습니다

en: Yagoda is ambitious, murderous, and if I was going to point the finger to anybody who possibly
ko: 야고다는 야심차고 살인적인 인물이었죠

en: had Dzerzhinsky whacked, it would be him.
ko: 제르진스키를 죽였을 가능성이 있는 인물을 지목한다면 그일 겁니다

en: And for the purposes simply of advancement.
ko: 순전히 승진을 위해서요

en: That's the, you know, the person to look out at any kind of corporate organization is your
ko: 어떤 조직에서든 경계해야 할 사람은

en: immediate subordinate.
ko: 어떤 조직에서든 경계해야 할 사람은

en: The person who could move into your job, because more than likely that's exactly what they're
ko: 직속 부하입니다 당신 자리로 올라올 수 있는 사람이니까

en: planning to do. Yeah, just one step away from the very top.
ko: 그럴 계획일 가능성이 높죠 꼭대기 바로 아래 있는 사람이

en: Somebody there will probably accumulate the most power.
ko: 가장 많은 권력을 축적할 겁니다
